This card explores the distributional shape of each real-valued predictor. Any integer variables, which are typically counts, will be ignored. Be on the look out for integer variables that have become real-values through normalisation.

The distributional analysis is achieved by charting each variable in a 2D "distribution space", characterised by skewness and kurtosis. This space can divided into regions that suggest certain data distributions are responsible for the data. This is the idea behind [Pearson Distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearson_distribution). To get charts similar to many of the published ones, there is an advanced setting to use skewness squared.

A Pearson distributions chart is shown below. This card does not follow this colour coding of regions and instead seeks to show how variables cluster in this space. Variables that cluster might have similar generation processes.

![](data:image/png;base64,){.alignleft}

However the colouring does make make it clear that the boundaries are sharp and even distant points can be similar if they stay within the same colour. The grey zone represents infeasible distributions.

In the flip side of the card, the distributional parameters are tabulated for each relevant predictor. One of these parameters is the lambda value relating to [power transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_transform) which are used in the normalisation of skew. Removing skew mainly helps in the detection of outliers in downstream processes that assume symmetrical outlier behaviour. It may also assist certain types of linear modelling tasks.

In power transformations, lambda (λ) is the parameter used to determine the optimal transformation of numerical variables into a less skewed shape. A lambda of 1.00 means that an identity transform is recommended (i.e. no change). A lambda value of NA means that no optimal power transform could be found. This mainly helps in the detection of outliers in downstream processes that assume symmetrical outlier behaviour. It may also assist certain types of linear modelling tasks.

#### Goal

The goal of this card is to assess the distributional characteristics of the predictors.

-   Whether sets of variables have similar distributional characteristics.
-   What is the best-fit distributional model, based upon either AIC, BIC or log-likelihood methodologies.
-   What levels of power transform are required to reduce the skew to a more symmetric distribution.
-   The application of normalisations of the mean, standard deviation and skew through the use of recipe steps.

#### Search space

The search space for distributions is:

|        |           |          |          |            |
|--------|-----------|----------|----------|------------|
| beta   | betapr    | cauchy   | exp      | gamma      |
| ged    | gumbel    | invgamma | invgauss | invweibull |
| kumar  | laplace   | lgamma   | llogis   | lnorm      |
| logis  | logitnorm | lomax    | naka     | norm       |
| pareto | power     | rayleigh | sged     | snorm      |
| sstd   | std       | unif     | weibull  |            |

The best-fit distribution model can be found by hovering the mouse over each point in the chart or from the table.

#### Limitations

-   Only real-values predictors are explored (i.e. of type "double")

-   Categorical data is ignored. Power transforms do not apply to categorical data.

-   Power transforms are not implemented for variables whose data type is integer. Integer data is often "count" data and often follows a binomial distribution that is definitely not symmetrical.

-   The power transform used in this card is the Yeo-Johnson transform as it allows for both positive and negative values. For variables that are entirely greater than zero. the Box-Cox transform is viable. However this variation is not currently implemented. This is approach is explored in package [bestNormalize](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/bestNormalize/index.html)

#### 

### Actions

Below are some actions that follow from a better understanding of the distributional structure of the predictors.

-   Gain understanding of the data distributions.
-   Finding sets of similar predictors.
-   Implement variable normalisations where appropriate. A button exists for applying centering, scaling and de-skewing transforms. Applying the transform should show the charted points moving closer together in the various charts.
